summary: I grew up in Sudan, where my family faced terrible hardships. The government took everything from my mother, burned down our houses, and killed many men including my father. The war lasted for many years, and life was very difficult, especially in Darfur where I am from. Despite the struggles, I still feel a connection to Sudan and hope one day to return when the country is better.

After the violence in Sudan, my family and I fled to Chad, where we had almost nothing and lived under trees for shelter. We stayed in refugee camps there for about five years, relying on help from the United Nations. Life was hard because the Chadian government didn’t want us to leave, and resources were scarce. Many people there struggled to find work or food, and some teenagers traveled to dangerous places like gold mines near Niger and Libya.
cues: gold mining near Niger and Libya

summary: I belong to the Masalit tribe, which the Sudanese government targeted during the conflict. The government favored Arabs and gave them weapons to attack us, leading to what I call genocide. Our people suffered greatly, with many killed and our villages destroyed. The Masalit identity is tied closely to the Darfur region, and we have endured years of violence because of it.

Darfur is where I am from, and it has been the center of so much suffering. The government there burned villages, killed many people including students, and destroyed our way of life. Education was nearly impossible because if you were from Darfur and went to the capital for college, you risked being killed. The war in Darfur lasted many years and left the region devastated.

The United Nations helped us when we fled to Chad by providing tents and food in the refugee camps. They also arranged for some of the sick and elderly to move to other countries, including my family. However, after some time, the UN stopped relocating refugees due to conflicts with the Chadian government. Their support was crucial during those early years in the camps.

Niger is near the area where some of our teenage boys traveled to work in gold mining. This desert region borders Chad and Libya, and it’s very dangerous because the Libyan government bombs the area. Many young men died there trying to find work to support their families. It’s a harsh reality for people from our refugee camps.

Libya borders the gold mining area where many of our young men went to find work. Unfortunately, the Libyan government does not approve of this and has bombed the mining sites, causing deaths among the workers. This has made it even harder for refugees and displaced people to survive. The violence in Libya affects us even though we are not there.

Our people follow Islam and the teachings of the Qur’an, which is the religion of almost the whole country in the North. We celebrate two big holidays, Eid al-Fitr and Eid al-Adha, each lasting seven days with family gatherings and prayers. Even here in the United States, we continue to celebrate Eid, though not as long or as festively as back home because there are fewer Muslims here.

South Sudan separated from Sudan after a long war between the Christian South and Muslim North. The conflict intensified when Omar al-Bashir became president about 32 years ago, leading to violence against Christians in the North. South Sudan is now its own country, distinct from where I am from in North Sudan. The division was a major event in our country’s history.

Christianity is the main religion in South Sudan, which separated from the Muslim North after a long and violent conflict. Before the war, Christians and Muslims lived peacefully together and even intermarried. But when Omar al-Bashir became president, he encouraged violence against Christians, which led to the division of the country. This religious divide shaped much of the recent history of Sudan.

Omar al-Bashir became president of Sudan about 32 years ago, and his leadership marked the start of intense conflict between the North and South. He favored Arabs and Muslims and encouraged violence against Christians, which led to many deaths and the eventual split of Sudan into two countries. His presidency brought a lot of suffering to my people, especially in Darfur and the South.

Eid al-Fitr is one of the two big holidays we celebrate in Sudan, lasting seven days. It starts with a special prayer on the first day, followed by family visits, gift-giving, and celebrations throughout the week. Back home, everyone participates and it’s a very festive time, but here in the U.S. we celebrate more quietly because there are fewer Muslims around.

Eid al-Adha is the other major holiday we celebrate for seven days in Sudan. Like Eid al-Fitr, it involves prayers, visiting family and friends, and sharing food and gifts. It’s a time when guests are treated like royalty and everyone comes together to celebrate. Though we still observe it here in the U.S., it’s not as lively as back home because the Muslim community is smaller.
Taiwan was not mentioned in my story or experiences, so I do not have any connection or memories related to Taiwan.
